verdict.reasoning:
All seven .planning/codebase/ uppercase map files (STRUCTURE.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, STACK.md, INTEGRATIONS.md, CONCERNS.md, TESTING.md, CONVENTIONS.md) are dated 2026-04-09 and contain extensive screenalytics references treating the repo as a live first-class runtime component. The screenalytics/ directory does not exist at the workspace root. Concrete evidence:

1. STRUCTURE.md line 10: "`├── screenalytics/` # ML pipeline, Screenalytics API, operator UIs..." — screenalytics appears 38 times, including lines 45-73 (8 directory-purpose blocks) and lines 117-119 (entry points screenalytics/apps/api/main.py, screenalytics/apps/workspace-ui/streamlit_app.py, screenalytics/scripts/dev.sh).

2. ARCHITECTURE.md lines 37-49 define "Screenalytics API and Pipeline Layer" and "Screenalytics Operator UI Layer" as active architecture layers with concrete file paths. Lines 68-73 define a "Screenalytics Metadata Sync Flow" as an active data flow. screenalytics appears 27 times.

3. STACK.md lines 8-9 list screenalytics/.python-version as a live Python version source, lines 41-46 document the Celery+Redis/Streamlit/Next.js screenalytics stack as active, line 57 lists screenalytics/requirements-core.in as a live FastAPI version source. screenalytics appears 37 times.

4. INTEGRATIONS.md has 33 screenalytics references, CONCERNS.md 14, TESTING.md 21, CONVENTIONS.md 26 — all presenting screenalytics as a live runtime.

5. The live runtime scripts (dev-workspace.sh, test-fast.sh, test.sh, AGENTS.md) each return 0 screenalytics references. The only live mention is Makefile line 260: "local screenalytics infra is retired; nothing to tear down."

6. The screenalytics-map-and-cleanup.md (dated 2026-05-28, inside the same .planning/codebase/ directory) explicitly documents the retirement: "screenalytics/ is an adjacent, ignored checkout/runtime folder inside the TRR workspace. It is not tracked by the TRR root repo." The newer replacement maps (backend-map.md, app-map.md, workspace-map.md) contain no dates but are demonstrably post-retirement.

The severity adjustment from High to Medium is appropriate because these are planning/documentation artifacts not runtime code — they cannot cause a runtime failure or security issue by themselves. However, since AGENTS.md points to .planning/ as an active planning/execution resource for agents, stale maps presenting a retired system as active could misdirect agent work, making Medium the right call rather than Low.
verdict.evidence: STRUCTURE.md:10 `├── screenalytics/ # ML pipeline, Screenalytics API, operator UIs...` (38 occurrences total); ARCHITECTURE.md:37-49 "Screenalytics API and Pipeline Layer" and "Screenalytics Operator UI Layer" as active layers (27 occurrences); STACK.md:41-46 Celery/Redis/Streamlit/Next.js listed as active screenalytics stack (37 occurrences); INTEGRATIONS.md:28 "Celery + Redis remain active inside screenalytics for queue-backed processing" (33 occurrences); `ls -d /Users/thomashulihan/Projects/TRR/screenalytics` → No such file or directory; Makefile:260 "local screenalytics infra is retired; nothing to tear down"; screenalytics-map-and-cleanup.md (dated 2026-05-28): "screenalytics/ is an adjacent, ignored checkout/runtime folder inside the TRR workspace. It is not tracked by the TRR root repo"; dev-workspace.sh/test-fast.sh/test.sh/AGENTS.md each return 0 screenalytics grep hits.

findings.title: Generated maps document the wrong cross-repo implementation order (TRR-Backend -> screenalytics -> TRR-APP)
findings.description: CONVENTIONS.md line 115 instructs: 'Land cross-repo contract work in this order: TRR-Backend first, screenalytics second, TRR-APP last.' ARCHITECTURE.md line 177 repeats it. The authoritative AGENTS.md now defines only a two-repo order: 'Backend-first for schema, API, auth, and shared contract changes' and 'App follow-through happens in the same session after backend contract changes land' (AGENTS.md:9-11) — there is no screenalytics step. This is a directly contradictory process instruction that would mislead an agent or contributor following the maps.
findings.recommendation: Remove the 'screenalytics second' step from both maps to match AGENTS.md's backend-first / app-follow-through order, or regenerate.
findings.evidence: CONVENTIONS.md:115 `Land cross-repo contract work in this order: TRR-Backend first, screenalytics second, TRR-APP last.`; AGENTS.md:9-11 `## Cross-Repo Implementation Order / - Backend-first ... / - App follow-through happens in the same session`

verdict.reasoning: Both cited files exist and contain the exact text claimed. CONVENTIONS.md:115 reads: "Land cross-repo contract work in this order: `TRR-Backend` first, `screenalytics` second, `TRR-APP` last." ARCHITECTURE.md:177 reads: "`TRR-Backend` first for schema/API/auth, `screenalytics` second for consumers/pipeline behavior, and `TRR-APP` last for UI/admin integration." The authoritative AGENTS.md:9-11 defines only two steps — backend-first, then app follow-through — with no screenalytics step. Screenalytics is confirmed retired: the live directory does not exist; the project is archived at TRR-screenalytics-retired-archive-20260528, and .planning/codebase/screenalytics-retirement-migration-plan.md explicitly describes its retirement. The planning maps were generated before or concurrent with retirement and were never updated to remove the stale three-repo order. The contradiction is real and would mislead any agent consuming these maps as authoritative routing instructions. Severity is adjusted to Medium rather than High because the consequence is agent confusion or wasted steps (attempting work in a non-existent repo), not a security or correctness failure.

findings.title: env-contract.md is stored with 0600 permissions despite being generated, non-secret content
findings.description: docs/workspace/env-contract.md is mode 0600 (`-rw-------`), unlike its sibling generated docs (env-deprecations.md, env-contract-inventory.md, shared-env-manifest.json are 0644). The file's own header states it is generated by `scripts/workspace-env-contract.sh` and it deliberately documents only env names/defaults, never secret values ('Do not use secret-bearing values as application names'). The restrictive mode is harmless to readers on this machine but is inconsistent with the other generated contract docs and can cause confusing access behavior in CI or for other tools/users; a regeneration that does not reset perms will perpetuate the drift.

reviews.summary: TRR-APP/apps/web is on a current stack (Next 16.1.6, React 19.1, TS 5.9, Tailwind 4) with several things done well: async params/searchParams are correctly migrated to the Next 15+ Promise pattern (273 files), route handlers consistently enforce auth via requireAdmin/route-factory helpers, pg uses Vercel's attachDatabasePool, and an enforced ESLint policy keeps `: any` and `@ts-ignore` at zero in src. However, the app is heavily client-rendered and effect-driven (1968 useState / 568 useEffect, near-zero React 19 API adoption), ships a 16,907-line single client page component, defines no App Router error/loading/not-found boundaries at all, and the global host-isolation middleware is silently dead because it lives in `src/proxy.ts` instead of `middleware.ts` (compiled middleware-manifest is empty). Dependency hygiene has a firebase-admin major-version split (root v13 vs app v12) and three coexisting eslint-plugin-react-hooks versions while the React Compiler correctness lints are turned off.

findings.title: Global host-isolation middleware is dead code: logic lives in src/proxy.ts, not middleware.ts (Next.js never runs it)
findings.description: src/proxy.ts is a 1024-line file that exports a Next.js-middleware-shaped function `proxy(request: NextRequest): NextResponse` plus `export const config = { matcher: ["/:path*"] }`. But Next.js only loads middleware from a file named `middleware.ts`/`src/middleware.ts` exporting a function the framework recognizes — a file named proxy.ts exporting `proxy` is never registered. Verified: no middleware.ts exists anywhere (find for middleware.* returns nothing), nothing re-exports proxy as middleware, no build step renames it, and the compiled .next/server/middleware-manifest.json contains `"middleware": {}` and `"sortedMiddleware": []` (empty). The proxy implements a defense-in-depth host-isolation control (returns 403 'Admin API is not available on this host.' / 'Admin origin is not configured.' when admin UI/API paths are requested on a non-admin host, gated by ADMIN_ENFORCE_HOST defaulting true). All of that never executes. Actual admin auth still works because every admin route calls requireAdmin (directly or via route factories — verified), so this is not an auth bypass, but the host-segregation hardening the team wrote is silently disabled and 1000+ lines read as load-bearing while being dead.
findings.recommendation: Decide intent and make it explicit. If host isolation is wanted: rename to src/middleware.ts and rename the export to the middleware entry Next expects (export default or `export function middleware`), keeping `export const config = { matcher: [...] }`, then rebuild and confirm middleware-manifest.json is non-empty. If it was intentionally retired in favor of per-route requireAdmin, delete src/proxy.ts and the stale .next/server/middleware.js to remove the misleading dead code. Add a tiny test asserting middleware-manifest.json is non-empty (or absent by design) so this can't silently regress.
findings.evidence: src/proxy.ts:1024 `export function proxy(request: NextRequest): NextResponse {`; :1120 `export const config = {`; :1063 `return NextResponse.json({ error: "Admin API is not available on this host." }, { status: 403 });`. .next/server/middleware-manifest.json => `{ "version": 3, "middleware": {}, "functions": {}, "sortedMiddleware": [] }`. `find apps/web -iname 'middleware.*'` (src) returns nothing.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding rests on the assumption that Next.js only recognizes `middleware.ts` and that `proxy.ts` is therefore dead code. This is false for the version of Next.js in use (16.1.6), which introduced `proxy.ts` as a first-class, intentional replacement for `middleware.ts`.

Key evidence:

1. **`PROXY_FILENAME = 'proxy'` is a first-class Next.js 16 constant.** Confirmed at `/node_modules/.pnpm/next@16.1.6_.../next/dist/lib/constants.js`: `const PROXY_FILENAME = 'proxy';` and `const PROXY_LOCATION_REGEXP = '(?:src/)?proxy';`. Next.js 16 detects `src/proxy.ts` via `proxyDetectionRegExp` during the build.

2. **The production build registered proxy.ts as Node.js middleware.** `/Users/thomashulihan/Projects/TRR/TRR-APP/apps/web/.next/server/functions-config-manifest.json` contains: `"/_middleware": { "runtime": "nodejs", "matchers": [{ "regexp": "^...$", "originalSource": "/:path*" }] }`. This is exactly the production registration of `proxy.ts` as an active Node.js middleware handler matching all routes.

3. **The empty `middleware-manifest.json` is not evidence of dead code.** In Next.js 16, `middleware-manifest.json` is for Edge Runtime middleware only. Node.js runtime proxy files are registered in `functions-config-manifest.json` under `/_middleware`. The build code confirms: `functionsConfigManifest.functions['/_middleware'] = { runtime: 'nodejs', matchers: ... }`.

4. **The Turbopack dev build explicitly picks up `src/proxy.ts`.** The dev `middleware.js` contains: `INNER_MIDDLEWARE_MODULE => "[project]/apps/web/src/proxy.ts [middleware] (ecmascript)"`, showing Turbopack treats proxy.ts as the middleware entry point.

5. **Next.js 16's middleware template calls `mod.proxy` (not `mod.default` or `mod.middleware`).** `/node_modules/.pnpm/next@16.1.6_.../next/dist/esm/build/templates/middleware.js`: `const handlerUserland = (isProxy ? mod.proxy : mod.middleware) || mod.default;`. The named export `export function proxy(...)` at `src/proxy.ts:1024` is exactly what the framework calls.

6. **Next.js 16 deprecates `middleware.ts` in favor of `proxy.ts`.** The build emits a warning: `The "middleware" file convention is deprecated. Please use "proxy" instead.` Having a `proxy.ts` without a `middleware.ts` is the correct, current pattern.

The finding's evidence of an empty `middleware-manifest.json` is a misinterpretation of Next.js 16 internals. The proxy logic in `src/proxy.ts` is active, registered, and executed on every request in both dev (Turbopack) and production builds.

findings.title: Backend origin (TRR_API_URL) interpolated into client-facing JSON error bodies across 10 admin routes
findings.description: Ten admin proxy routes embed the raw internal backend origin into the JSON error payload returned to the browser, e.g. refresh-images returns `{ error: 'Backend fetch failed', detail: `${detail} (TRR_API_URL=${rawBackendUrl})` }` with status 502. This leaks the internal backend host/URL (Render/Modal origin) to any client that can trigger the error. These are admin-gated routes (requireAdmin enforced), so exposure is limited to authenticated admins, hence High not Critical — but internal infrastructure topology should never be returned in a response body; it belongs only in server logs (which these routes also already do via console.error).
findings.recommendation: Return a generic, stable error message to the client (e.g. 'Backend request failed; check server logs') and move the TRR_API_URL/backendHint into the server-side console.error/log only. A shared error-formatting helper already exists in some routes (formatFetchProxyError) — centralize all 10 on it and strip env values from the client-visible `error`/`detail` fields.
findings.evidence: grep `TRR_API_URL=` in src/app/api returns 10 files. refresh-images route.ts:153 `{ error: "Backend fetch failed", detail: `${detail} (TRR_API_URL=${rawBackendUrl})` }, { status: 502 }`; shows/[showId]/news/route.ts:32 `error: `Backend request failed while loading unified news (TRR_API_URL=${backendHint})...``. Others: networks-streaming/sync, shows/sync-from-lists, shows/[showId]/refresh(+retry), auto-count-images, bravo/videos/sync-thumbnails, google-news/sync(+[jobId]).

verdict.reasoning: All 10 cited files exist and each contains exactly one line that interpolates `process.env.TRR_API_URL` (or a variable derived from it) directly into a JSON error response body returned to the browser. The primary cited file is `/Users/thomashulihan/Projects/TRR/TRR-APP/apps/web/src/app/api/admin/trr-api/people/[personId]/refresh-images/route.ts` at line 153. The exact code at line 89 captures `rawBackendUrl = process.env.TRR_API_URL ?? "unset"` and line 153 returns `{ error: "Backend fetch failed", detail: \`${detail} (TRR_API_URL=${rawBackendUrl})\` }` with HTTP 502. The same pattern appears at lines 173, 86, 94, 103, 32, 98, 31, 38, and 37 in the nine other routes. All 10 routes do call `await requireAdmin(request)` before reaching the error path, limiting exposure to authenticated admins. The server-side `console.error` also captures this information, so returning it to the client provides no additional debugging value. The severity rating of High (not Critical) is correctly calibrated given the admin-only access gate — the internal backend origin (Render/Modal host) is leaked to admins only, not to the public.

findings.title: 16,907-line single 'use client' page component with 199 useState and 51 useEffect calls
findings.description: The admin show detail route is one client component of 16,907 lines holding ~199 useState and ~51 useEffect hooks. This is a severe maintainability and performance liability: the entire route is client-rendered (no server-component data fetching), every state change risks wide re-renders across a huge tree, and the whole module ships to the browser. PersonPageClient.tsx (11,946), reddit-sources-manager.tsx (10,150), SocialAccountProfilePage.tsx (10,099) and season-social-analytics-section.tsx (8,662) are in the same class. This concentration also explains the heavy reliance on effects and the disabling of React Compiler lints (see related finding).
findings.recommendation: Split by tab/section into separate client components (the file already imports many show-tab components — push the remaining inline logic out the same way), and lift read-only data loading into the server component layer or React Query-style hooks instead of useEffect+fetch. Co-locate state with the subtree that uses it to shrink re-render scope. Target getting each route component under ~1-2k lines. Even incremental extraction of the largest tabs will materially cut the client bundle and re-render cost.
findings.evidence: `wc -l` => 16907 src/app/admin/trr-shows/[showId]/page.tsx (largest tsx in src). Line 1 `"use client";`. In-file `grep -c useState` => 199, `grep -c useEffect` => 51. Next four largest are also 8k-12k-line client components.

verdict.reasoning:
Every metric in the finding is verified against the actual file:

1. File exists: /Users/thomashulihan/Projects/TRR/TRR-APP/apps/web/src/app/admin/trr-shows/[showId]/page.tsx
2. Line count: `wc -l` returns exactly 16,907.
3. Line 1 is `"use client";` — confirmed by direct Read.
4. `grep -c "useState"` returns 199; `grep -c "useEffect"` returns 51 — both match claimed counts.
5. The default export `TrrShowDetailPage` begins at line 2345, meaning ~14,500 lines of the file are inside or below that single function. Lines 1–2344 contain helper sub-components and utility functions that are also fully inlined in the same module.
6. All four secondary files cited exist at the claimed line counts: PersonPageClient.tsx (11,946), reddit-sources-manager.tsx (10,150), SocialAccountProfilePage.tsx (10,099), season-social-analytics-section.tsx (8,662).

The severity rating of High is justified. A 16,907-line single `use client` boundary with 199 useState and 51 useEffect hooks in one module is a confirmed, severe architectural issue: the entire component tree is client-rendered with no server-component data fetching boundary, every state update risks wide re-renders across a huge virtual DOM subtree, and the full module ships to the browser. This is not dead or test-only code — it is the active admin show detail route.

findings.title: No App Router error.tsx / loading.tsx / not-found.tsx / global-error.tsx boundaries anywhere (172 pages)
findings.line: n/a (find across src/app returns zero matches)
findings.description: Across 172 page.tsx files there are zero error.tsx, loading.tsx, not-found.tsx, global-error.tsx, or template.tsx files. The app relies solely on a single custom class-component ErrorBoundary wrapped around {children} in the root layout. That client ErrorBoundary cannot catch errors thrown in Server Components / async data loading, provides no per-segment recovery (`reset()`), and there are no Suspense-based loading.tsx streaming fallbacks (only 9 ad-hoc <Suspense> uses app-wide). Result: a thrown error in any route bubbles to a generic boundary or an unstyled Next default, and route transitions show no skeleton/loading UI. This is a core App Router convention left unused.
findings.recommendation: Add at minimum a root app/error.tsx (client, with reset) and app/global-error.tsx, plus app/not-found.tsx. Add loading.tsx (or Suspense boundaries) for the heavy admin segments (trr-shows/[showId], people/[personId], social) so navigation streams a skeleton instead of blocking. Keep the existing ErrorBoundary for intra-page client widgets, but stop treating it as the app's only error strategy.
findings.evidence: `find src/app -iname error.tsx -o -iname loading.tsx -o -iname not-found.tsx -o -iname global-error.tsx -o -iname template.tsx` => no output. src/app/layout.tsx wraps children in a single <ErrorBoundary> (client class component, componentDidCatch only console.errors). `grep -rln Suspense src/app` => 9 files only.

verdict.reasoning:
Every factual claim in the finding is verified against actual code:

1. Zero App Router special files: `find /Users/thomashulihan/Projects/TRR/TRR-APP/apps/web/src/app -iname "error.tsx" -o -iname "loading.tsx" -o -iname "not-found.tsx" -o -iname "global-error.tsx" -o -iname "template.tsx"` returned no output — confirmed.

2. 172 page.tsx files: `find ... -iname "page.tsx" | wc -l` = 172 — confirmed.

3. Single root ErrorBoundary: `layout.tsx` lines 62-65 wrap `{children}` in a single `<ErrorBoundary>` client class component — confirmed.

4. ErrorBoundary is a class component that cannot catch Server Component / async data errors: `/Users/thomashulihan/Projects/TRR/TRR-APP/apps/web/src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx` is a `"use client"` class component with `componentDidCatch` that only calls `console.error`. Recovery is `window.location.reload()`, not the Next.js App Router `reset()` callback. It has no awareness of route segments. — confirmed.

5. Sparse Suspense: grep returns 9 files — confirmed.

The severity rated as High is justified. A thrown error in any Server Component (async data fetch, DB call, etc.) will bypass this class-based ErrorBoundary entirely, because React class error boundaries cannot catch errors in async server-rendered subtrees. The result is Next.js's built-in unstyled error page or a blank screen, with no route-level recovery or streaming fallbacks. The gap is real and material for a 172-page app. Severity is not inflated.

findings.title: firebase-admin major-version split across the workspace (root ^13.7.0 vs apps/web ^12.7.0) plus pinned @firebase/* hoisting workaround
findings.line: deps: firebase ^12.10.0, firebase-admin ^12.7.0, @firebase/* exact pins; root package.json: firebase ^12.11.0, firebase-admin ^13.7.0
findings.description: The repo-root package.json declares firebase ^12.11.0 and firebase-admin ^13.7.0, while apps/web/package.json declares firebase ^12.10.0 and firebase-admin ^12.7.0 — a full major-version mismatch on firebase-admin (12 vs 13) between the two manifests that both participate in the pnpm workspace. firebase-admin v13 dropped Node 18 support and changed some APIs vs v12, so which one resolves at runtime is fragile. Separately, apps/web pins five internal packages (@firebase/app 0.14.9, @firebase/component 0.7.1, @firebase/firestore 4.9.0, @firebase/util 1.14.0, @firebase/logger 0.5.0) to exact versions specifically to defeat pnpm hoisting (per the webpack-alias block in next.config.ts that forces a single copy or Firestore throws 'Service firestore is not available'). Hand-pinning internals to specific patch versions is brittle: a firebase minor bump will desync these pins and reintroduce the dual-copy bug.
findings.recommendation: Align firebase-admin to a single major across root and apps/web (decide 12 or 13 deliberately; 13 if on Node 24 as engines declare). Prefer a pnpm catalog or root-level override for firebase/firebase-admin so versions can't drift between manifests. Re-evaluate whether the five @firebase/* exact pins + next.config alias are still needed on current firebase 12.x; if so, add a pnpm override/`peerDependencyRules` to keep them single-versioned automatically instead of manual exact pins, and add a check that fails CI if @firebase/* dedupe breaks.
findings.evidence: apps/web/package.json: `"firebase": "^12.10.0"`, `"firebase-admin": "^12.7.0"`, `"@firebase/firestore": "4.9.0"` (+4 exact pins). root package.json: `"firebase": "^12.11.0"`, `"firebase-admin": "^13.7.0"`. next.config.ts firebaseInternals alias block comment: 'Without this, pnpm can hoist different versions ... causing "Service firestore is not available".'

verdict.reasoning:
Every specific claim in the finding is verified by direct file evidence.

1. apps/web/package.json (lines 48-52, 57-58) declares exactly: "firebase": "^12.10.0", "firebase-admin": "^12.7.0", and five exact @firebase/* pins: "@firebase/app": "0.14.9", "@firebase/component": "0.7.1", "@firebase/firestore": "4.9.0", "@firebase/logger": "0.5.0", "@firebase/util": "1.14.0".

2. Root TRR-APP/package.json (lines 4-5) declares "firebase": "^12.11.0" and "firebase-admin": "^13.7.0" — a full major version ahead of apps/web on firebase-admin.

3. pnpm-lock.yaml confirms both firebase-admin@12.7.0 and firebase-admin@13.7.0 are concretely resolved and installed as separate packages (lines 4348/4352 in the packages section; lines 12160/12178 in the snapshots section). This is not a theoretical risk — two copies are physically installed.

4. next.config.ts (lines 21-52) contains the firebaseInternals alias block exactly as described, with the comment "Without this, pnpm can hoist different versions of @firebase/component and the Firestore service factory registers on a different container than the one the app instance uses, causing 'Service firestore is not available'."

5. firebase-admin is actively imported in production server routes (/src/lib/firebaseAdmin.ts, /src/lib/server/auth.ts, /src/app/api/games/report-problem/route.ts), confirming this is live production code, not dead or test-only.

6. pnpm-workspace.yaml declares packages: ["apps/*"], confirming the root and apps/web both participate in the same workspace, making the dual-manifest split a real resolver concern.

The finding's description of the brittleness of hand-pinning five internal @firebase/* packages to exact patch versions is also accurate — any firebase minor bump that changes internal sub-package versions will desync those pins and could reintroduce the dual-copy Firestore registration bug the alias block was added to solve.

The High severity is appropriate: two major versions of a security-critical server-side library (firebase-admin, used for auth token verification and Firestore admin access) are concurrently installed in the workspace, and the mitigating alias workaround only covers Webpack builds — Turbopack (used via `dev` and `dev:turbo` scripts) has its own resolver that may not respect the same aliases.
